next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Beni had forty minutes between getting home and the time Eszter said she would be at the chicken shop on West Green Road. Forty minutes was enough to strip the blanket off the sofa bed, shake the stale sleep out of it at the window, and learn again that damp plaster and old frying oil were stronger than lemon spray from the pound shop. He shoved his Mercer tie into the drawer under the television stand, kicked Laci's spare work boots behind the armchair, and folded yesterday's shirt so its collar stains did not show. The room still looked like what it was: a front room rented as a bedroom because London could always find one more way to charge a man for standing upright indoors.

The buses made the glass shiver every few minutes. Somebody downstairs was frying something sweet with too much cinnamon. Somewhere in the building a child cried in short outraged bursts, stopped, started again. Beni stood in the middle of the room with the blanket in his hands and tried to decide whether he had made it better or only arranged the embarrassment into straighter lines.

At noon, in the Ashdown basement kitchen, Eszter had said it like an instruction.

"I'm finishing at six. There's a place near Seven Sisters that panics before closing and drops the chicken price. I'll get one. You buy potatoes and sour cream. Don't do anything stupid like light candles."

He had looked up from the slice of bread he was eating. "Why would I light candles?"

"Because you're nineteen and frightened."

"I'm not frightened."

"Then don't clean too hard either. I hate entering a room that look...
open_hooks_to_carry: Make Jonas's studio feel materially different from the Ashdown house: messy, performative, and no less status-driven.
Show Nina's dependence on Beni growing because he solves problems without moral commentary.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...k in Belgravia was done. If he left immediately he could get to Tottenham before the buses filled, maybe beat Laci home, maybe eat something cheap enough to count as sensible. There was no practical reason to stay another minute.

Still he kept sitting there, listening to the engine tick itself cooler.

The stew smell had dried faintly into his cuffs. When he lifted his hand to his mouth he could still catch tea and paprika and the clean soap from Eszter's sink under the leather and stale perfume of the car. The house behind him was the same house as before: polished rooms upstairs, service doors below, rich people moving their needs through other people's bodies. Nothing there had become safe. Nothing there belonged to him.

But now the basement had a chair that had held him warm for an hour, a child laughing through a phone speaker, a woman who asked questions as if the answers might matter. It was not much. He knew better than to call not much by a bigger name.

He took the empty paper cup from the holder, though even that excuse was thin, and went back through the service door.

The kitchen light was still on. Eszter was at the counter with a pencil stuck through her hair now, writing a shopping list on the back of a delivery invoice. She looked up when he came in and took in his face, his empty hands, the fact that he was already done pretending he had business.

"What?" she said.

He shut the door behind him. "You said tea."

For a second she only stared. Then her mouth bent, unwilling and real.

"I did," she said. "Sit down before I regret being hospitable."

He sat.
state_deltas_required: Deliver Nina's forgotten pills and catalogue to Jonas and return without giving Oscar a reason to investigate.
Beni lies for Nina for the first time, crossing from passive discretion into active complicity.
